perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary Information must be retained by the Recipient in accordance with its customary procedures for handling confidential information of this nature and disclosed only to the Recipient's Affiliates or employees (or , attorneys, accountants and agents who have a non-disclosure obligation at least as restrictive as this Agreement) who need to know such information for purposes of the servicing or protection of its interest in respect of the Loans, the Credit Agreement and the Loan Documents (each as defined under the Credit Agreement) and the transactions contemplated thereby and to such third parties as the Discloser has consented to by prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: All Proprietary Information must be retained by the Recipient using procedures it deems appropriate for handling confidential information and disclosed only to individuals it considers relevant, including Affiliates or employees (or, attorneys, accountants, and agents with a non-disclosure obligation no less restrictive than this Agreement) with a perceived need to know such information for servicing or protecting its interests related to the Loans, the Credit Agreement, and Loan Documents (as defined in the Credit Agreement) and transactions. Disclosure to other third parties may occur when deemed suitable by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'procedures it deems appropriate,' 'individuals it considers relevant,' 'perceived need to know,' and 'when deemed suitable by the Recipient' introduce significant ambiguity regarding the standards and criteria for protecting and disclosing confidential information. This makes it subjective and difficult to enforce a consistent standard of care.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri Revised Statutes § 417.453 outlines requirements related to trade secret protection, indicating that reasonable efforts must be made to maintain secrecy. The modified text, with its ambiguous wording, could be interpreted as not meeting the 'reasonable efforts' standard required under Missouri law for trade secret protection, as it leaves the level of protection largely to the Recipient's discretion.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 417.453 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 417 < > • Effective - 28 Aug 1995 417.453. Definitions. — As used in sections 417.450 to 417.467 , the following terms mean: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" : (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of a person by another person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of a person without express or implied consent by another person who: a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; or c. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) "Person" , a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity, whether for profit or not for profit; (4) "Trade secret" , information, including but not limited to, technical or nontechnical data, a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by p
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity that undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard required for trade secret protection under Missouri law. The original text mandates adherence to 'customary procedures,' while the changed text uses subjective terms like 'procedures it deems appropriate' and 'individuals it considers relevant,' which are open to interpretation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and notes that trade secrets must not be disclosed or used without consent, implying a duty to maintain secrecy. The modified text weakens this duty by allowing disclosure based on the Recipient's discretion, potentially conflicting with the legal requirement to make reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, all copies of written, recorded, graphical or other tangible Proprietary Information must either be returned to the Discloser, or destroyed (i) after the Recipient's need for it has expired or (ii) upon the request of the Discloser. At the request of the Discloser, the Recipient will furnish a certificate of an officer of the Recipient certifying that any Proprietary Information not returned to Discloser has been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, all copies of written, recorded, graphical or other tangible Proprietary Information should be returned to the Discloser when feasible, or destroyed according to the Recipient's internal data management policies. The Recipient will endeavor to provide a certificate, upon request, from a representative confirming the destruction of Proprietary Information not returned, subject to internal approval processes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must' to 'should when feasible', 'after the Recipient's need for it has expired' to 'according to the Recipient's internal data management policies,' and adding 'endeavor' and 'subject to internal approval processes' introduces ambiguity. The obligation to return or destroy becomes discretionary and dependent on internal policies, weakening the original requirement and creating uncertainty about compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: While concerning lease contracts, MRS § 400.2A-629 highlights the importance of clearly defined obligations upon termination of an agreement. By making the return or destruction of proprietary information conditional and subject to internal policies, the modified text introduces ambiguity that could be viewed as conflicting with the principle of clearly defined obligations at the end of a contractual agreement, potentially complicating the enforcement of proprietary information handling.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return or destroy proprietary information by using 'should when feasible' instead of 'must', and introducing 'according to the Recipient's internal data management policies,' 'endeavor,' and 'subject to internal approval processes'. This introduces ambiguity and makes compliance discretionary, conflicting with the principle of clearly defined obligations upon termination of an agreement. The Law Explanation highlights how this ambiguity could complicate enforcement of proprietary information handling. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant, the Law Explanation emphasizes the need for clear obligations at the end of an agreement which the change undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Each party warrants that it has the right to disclose all Proprietary Information which it will disclose to the other party pursuant to this Agreement, and each party agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the other from all claims by a third party related to the wrongful disclosure of such third party's information. Otherwise, neither party makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, with respect to any Proprietary Information. Neither party is liable for indirect, incidental, consequential, or punitive damages of any nature or kind resulting from or arising in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party believes it possesses the right to disclose the Proprietary Information shared under this Agreement, and both parties intend to indemnify and hold harmless each other from credible claims by a third party evidently related to the potentially wrongful disclosure of said third party's information. Otherwise, each party makes only reasonable representations and warranties, express or implied, with respect to any Proprietary Information. Neither party is liable for damages of any kind.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'warrants' with 'believes it possesses' weakens the assurance of rightful disclosure. Adding 'credible claims', 'evidently related,' and 'potentially wrongful' introduces subjectivity to the indemnification clause. Stating 'reasonable representations and warranties' instead of 'any representation or warranty' further dilutes the original guarantees and attempts to limit overall liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri Revised Statutes § 400.2-312 addresses warranty of title and against infringement in sales contracts. While this Confidentiality Agreement is not a sales contract, the principle of providing a clear warranty regarding the right to transfer information or goods without infringing on third-party rights is relevant. The modified text weakens the warranty and indemnification obligations, making it less clear who bears the risk if a third party claims infringement due to the disclosed information. This is in tension with the spirit of ensuring clear title and protection against infringement found in Missouri law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 400.2-312 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 400 < > • Effective - 28 Aug 1963 400.2-312. Warranty of title and against infringement — buyer's obligation against infringement. — (1) Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (a) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. ­­-------- (L. 1963 p. 503 § 2-312) ---- end of effective 28 Aug 1963 ---- use this link to bookmark section 400.2-312 Click here for the Reorganization Act of 1974 - or - Concurrent Resolutions Having Force & Effect of Law In accordance with Section 3.090 , the language of statutory sections enacted during a legislative session are updated and available on this website on the effective date of such enacted statutory section. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the assurance of rightful disclosure by changing 'warrants' to 'believes it possesses'. It introduces subjectivity to the indemnification clause with terms like 'credible claims', 'evidently related', and 'potentially wrongful'. Additionally, it dilutes the original guarantees by stating 'reasonable representations and warranties' instead of 'any representation or warranty'. According to Missouri Revised Statutes § 400.2-312, which discusses warranty of title and against infringement, the original contract provided a clear warranty regarding the right to transfer information. The modified text makes it less clear who bears the risk if a third party claims infringement, contradicting the principle of ensuring clear title and protection against infringement as it weakens the legal obligation. Snippet 1 states '...there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (a) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful...' which aligns with the original contract language. The change undermines this principle.
